insights.title: Total bank credit reached ₹213.6 lakh crore in April 2026, the highest level on record
insights.body: Total bank credit reached ₹213.6 lakh crore in April 2026, the highest level on record. Volume expanded ₹6.1 lakh crore from March → April, extending the upward trajectory from ₹178.7 lakh crore in February 2025.
insights.implication: Banks are deploying capital at record pace, signaling strong loan demand and institutional confidence in credit expansion.

insights.title: Bank credit growth accelerated 5.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, the maximum…
insights.body: Bank credit growth accelerated 5.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, the maximum acceleration in this dataset. Acceleration has held steady at 5.1pp across all six reporting periods from February 2025 through April 2026.
insights.implication: This sustained acceleration indicates structural momentum rather than cyclical uptick — banks have shifted into a higher growth regime.

insights.title: Banks added ₹29.6 lakh crore in absolute credit during FY26, matching the peak annual…
insights.body: Banks added ₹29.6 lakh crore in absolute credit during FY26, matching the peak annual expansion. The FY26 addition of ₹29.6 lakh crore has remained constant across all reporting periods since February 2025.
insights.implication: This represents the largest annual credit creation in the dataset, demonstrating banks' capacity for sustained large-scale lending.

insights.title: Bank credit grew 16.1% year-on-year in April 2026, the fastest growth rate in the…
insights.body: Bank credit grew 16.1% year-on-year in April 2026, the fastest growth rate in the four-period window. Growth accelerated from 14.6% → 16.1% between March and April, building on the 11.0% → 14.6% climb since April 2025.
insights.implication: Credit is expanding at the upper end of its historical range, suggesting robust demand across borrower segments without signs of cooling.

insights.title: Food credit outstanding fell to ₹70,271 crore in April 2026, down ₹12,372 crore from the…
insights.body: Food credit outstanding fell to ₹70,271 crore in April 2026, down ₹12,372 crore from the prior month. Volume declined from ₹82,643 crore → ₹70,271 crore, following the seasonal peak of ₹89,295 crore in February 2026.
insights.implication: Food credit shows typical seasonal volatility tied to agricultural cycles, with April representing the post-harvest reduction in working capital needs.

insights.title: Food credit grew 139.4% year-on-year in April 2026, the highest growth rate in the dataset
insights.body: Food credit grew 139.4% year-on-year in April 2026, the highest growth rate in the dataset. Growth surged from 58.9% → 139.4% between March and April, following a steady climb from 23.1% in February 2025.
insights.implication: Despite low absolute volumes, food credit is expanding at explosive rates, likely reflecting policy support for agricultural lending and seasonal working capital demand.

insights.title: Non-food credit reached ₹212.9 lakh crore in April 2026, setting a new record high
insights.body: Non-food credit reached ₹212.9 lakh crore in April 2026, setting a new record high. Volume expanded ₹6.2 lakh crore from ₹206.7 lakh crore → ₹212.9 lakh crore, up from ₹178.1 lakh crore in February 2025.
insights.implication: Non-food credit drives virtually all system growth, indicating broad-based demand across industrial, services, and retail segments.

insights.title: Non-food credit added ₹29.2 lakh crore during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this…
insights.body: Non-food credit added ₹29.2 lakh crore during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this dataset. The FY26 addition has remained constant at ₹29.2 lakh crore across all reporting periods since February 2025.
insights.implication: This represents the core engine of credit growth, with banks channeling the vast majority of new lending into commercial and retail sectors rather than food-related financing.

insights.title: Non-food credit grew 15.89% year-on-year in April 2026
insights.body: Non-food credit grew 15.89% year-on-year in April 2026. Growth accelerated from 14.45% → 15.89%, gaining 1.44 points over two months.
insights.implication: Credit expansion is at or near its highest level in this window, signaling strong lending momentum across Indian banks.

insights.title: All 4 main sectors recorded positive year-on-year growth in April 2026
insights.body: All 4 main sectors recorded positive year-on-year growth in April 2026. Breadth remained steady at 4 sectors, unchanged from the prior period.
insights.implication: Growth is broad-based across agriculture, industry, services, and retail — no sector is dragging down the overall expansion.

insights.title: Agriculture holds 12.43% of total bank credit, up 0.12 percentage points from the prior…
insights.body: Agriculture holds 12.43% of total bank credit, up 0.12 percentage points from the prior period. Share ticked up from 12.30% → 12.43%, staying near its historical median level.
insights.implication: Agriculture maintains its traditional ~12.4% allocation despite strong growth momentum — banks are expanding ag lending in line with overall portfolio expansion.

insights.title: Agriculture credit grew 15.70% year-on-year, the strongest pace in this 6-period dataset
insights.body: Agriculture credit grew 15.70% year-on-year, the strongest pace in this 6-period dataset. Growth accelerated from 12.33% → 15.70%, marking a +3.37 percentage point jump in one period.
insights.implication: This is agriculture's highest growth rate on record in our window — likely driven by strong crop cycles and renewed policy focus on rural lending.

insights.title: Industry credit shows full-year acceleration of 6.7 percentage points, holding at its…
insights.body: Industry credit shows full-year acceleration of 6.7 percentage points, holding at its maximum level. FY acceleration has remained steady at 6.7pp across all 6 periods in this dataset.
insights.implication: Industry lending has sustained its strongest structural momentum throughout this window — this isn't a cyclical spike but an entrenched uptrend.

insights.title: Industry accounts for 21.52% of total credit, essentially unchanged from the prior period
insights.body: Industry accounts for 21.52% of total credit, essentially unchanged from the prior period. Share edged up marginally from 21.51% → 21.52%, staying near historical median levels.
insights.implication: Industry maintains stable portfolio allocation despite strong growth — banks are scaling industrial lending proportionally with total book expansion.

insights.title: Industry credit grew 14.96% year-on-year, the highest growth rate in this 6-period window
insights.body: Industry credit grew 14.96% year-on-year, the highest growth rate in this 6-period window. Growth accelerated from 13.54% → 14.96%, building on an already strong upward trajectory.
insights.implication: Industry credit is hitting peak growth momentum — likely reflecting infrastructure investment, manufacturing recovery, and corporate capex revival.

insights.title: Personal loans represent 32.60% of total credit, down 0.28 percentage points from the…
insights.body: Personal loans represent 32.60% of total credit, down 0.28 percentage points from the prior period. Share declined from 32.88% → 32.60%, remaining the largest sector but losing ground marginally.
insights.implication: Personal loans still dominate but are ceding share to productive sectors — a healthy rebalancing toward agriculture and industry after years of retail-led growth.

insights.title: Personal loans credit grew 16.24% year-on-year in April 2026, the strongest reading in…
insights.body: Personal loans credit grew 16.24% year-on-year in April 2026, the strongest reading in the 6-period window. Growth accelerated from 15.22% to 16.24%, extending the upward trajectory from 11.7% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Consumer lending momentum is building after a slow start to FY26, suggesting household credit demand has turned durably positive.

insights.title: Services credit maintained 7.0 percentage point full-year acceleration in April 2026, the…
insights.body: Services credit maintained 7.0 percentage point full-year acceleration in April 2026, the maximum level across all periods. FY acceleration held steady at 7.0pp for six consecutive periods since February 2025.
insights.implication: Services credit growth has structurally shifted higher and sustained the pace, indicating broad-based economic activity beyond manufacturing.

insights.title: Services captured 28.47% of total bank credit in April 2026, the highest share in the…
insights.body: Services captured 28.47% of total bank credit in April 2026, the highest share in the dataset. Share rose from 28.10% to 28.47%, continuing the climb from 27.7% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Banks are allocating more capital to services as the sector outgrows traditional industry lending, reshaping credit deployment toward consumption and business services.

insights.title: Services credit grew 18.99% year-on-year in April 2026, the peak growth rate in the…
insights.body: Services credit grew 18.99% year-on-year in April 2026, the peak growth rate in the 6-period window. Growth jumped from 16.26% to 18.99%, accelerating from the 12.0% pace in April 2025.
insights.implication: Services is the economy's growth engine, with credit expansion nearly double the historical norm signaling strong business investment and working capital demand.

insights.title: Large corporates hold a 67.15% share of industry credit, up 0.02 points from the prior…
insights.body: Large corporates hold a 67.15% share of industry credit, up 0.02 points from the prior period. The share has stabilized around 67% after declining from 71.0% → 67.1% over the past year.
insights.implication: The corporate credit market has found equilibrium. Large enterprises maintain dominant access to bank financing, but the recent decline suggests banks are gradually expanding to smaller players.

insights.title: Large corporate credit growth accelerated to 8.95%, the strongest reading in this…
insights.body: Large corporate credit growth accelerated to 8.95%, the strongest reading in this 6-period window. Growth momentum built from 7.8% → 8.9% over two periods, breaking above the historical range.
insights.implication: Banks are backing large corporate capex plans with renewed confidence. This acceleration signals corporate India's investment cycle is gaining momentum with institutional financing support.

insights.title: Medium enterprises hold a 9.65% share of industry credit, down marginally by 0.02 points
insights.body: Medium enterprises hold a 9.65% share of industry credit, down marginally by 0.02 points. The share remains stable in the 9.7% range after climbing from 8.9% a year ago.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have secured a steady 1-in-10 share of industry financing. The stability suggests banks have found a sustainable allocation to this growth-critical segment.

insights.title: Micro & Small enterprise credit posted 24.2pp full-year acceleration — the highest…
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit posted 24.2pp full-year acceleration — the highest reading in this dataset. Acceleration held at the maximum level of 24.2pp, unchanged from prior period.
insights.implication: This signals entrenched momentum in small business lending, not a temporary spike. Banks are systematically expanding micro-enterprise exposure.

insights.title: Micro & Small enterprises claimed 23.20% of total industry credit — in the upper range of…
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprises claimed 23.20% of total industry credit — in the upper range of historical levels. Share held steady at 23.2%, up from 20.0% → 23.5% over the past year.
insights.implication: Small businesses are winning a structurally larger slice of bank credit allocation. This reflects deliberate portfolio rebalancing toward smaller ticket lending.

insights.title: Micro & Small enterprise credit surged 33.14% year-on-year — the fastest growth rate on…
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit surged 33.14% year-on-year — the fastest growth rate on record. Growth accelerated from 30.39% → 33.14%, extending the climb from single digits a year ago.
insights.implication: Banks are aggressively expanding small business books after years of tepid growth. This pace signals a fundamental shift in credit allocation strategy.

insights.title: MSME credit outpaced large corporate lending by 24.2 percentage points — in the upper…
insights.body: MSME credit outpaced large corporate lending by 24.2 percentage points — in the upper range of this series. The growth gap widened from 22.6pp → 24.2pp, up from just 2.8pp a year ago.
insights.implication: Banks are clearly favoring smaller borrowers over large corporates. This divergence suggests either risk concerns about large deals or deliberate small-ticket strategy.

gaps.title: Medium enterprise credit growth reached 21.74%, accelerating 0.75 points and ranking in…
gaps.body: Medium enterprise credit growth reached 21.74%, accelerating 0.75 points and ranking in the upper range of recent performance. Growth momentum has strengthened from 21.0% → 21.7% and consistently outpaces large corporates by 12+ percentage points.
gaps.implication: Banks are aggressively financing the missing middle of Indian industry. This growth differential suggests policy-driven lending is successfully reaching scale-up businesses ahead of large enterprises.

insights.title: Housing Finance Companies command 18.1% of NBFC credit while Public Financial…
insights.body: Housing Finance Companies command 18.1% of NBFC credit while Public Financial Institutions hold 16.9%. PFIs shifted status from active to strengthening, with a 1.2 percentage point gap between the two segments.
insights.implication: The NBFC sector shows balanced allocation between housing and public finance, with PFIs gaining momentum — watch for PFIs to challenge HFC dominance.

insights.title: Public Financial Institutions are growing at 52.5% while Housing Finance Companies expand…
insights.body: Public Financial Institutions are growing at 52.5% while Housing Finance Companies expand at 15.5%. A massive 37 percentage point growth spread separates the fastest and slowest NBFC segments.
insights.implication: This is an extreme divergence — PFIs are in hypergrowth mode while HFCs face structural headwinds or regulatory constraints.

insights.title: Shipping leads services growth at 42.2% and advanced to strengthening status, followed by…
insights.body: Shipping leads services growth at 42.2% and advanced to strengthening status, followed by Computer Software at 39.0% and NBFCs at 26.3%. Shipping moved from active to strengthening, while Computer Software, Trade, Aviation, and Other Services all shifted to strengthening status.
insights.implication: Growth is broadening across services with infrastructure, technology, and trade finance all accelerating simultaneously—indicating robust economic expansion rather than sector-specific bubbles.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% of trade credit and strengthened, while Retail Trade's 43.6%…
insights.body: Wholesale Trade holds 56.4% of trade credit and strengthened, while Retail Trade's 43.6% share weakened. The wholesale-retail split shifted toward wholesale, with wholesale strengthening and retail weakening in relative positioning.
insights.implication: B2B commerce is capturing more credit allocation than consumer retail, suggesting supply chain financing and inventory investment are driving trade credit more than end-consumer spending.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade grew 20.1% and Retail Trade 11.5%, both advancing to strengthening status…
insights.body: Wholesale Trade grew 20.1% and Retail Trade 11.5%, both advancing to strengthening status despite the 8.6 percentage point growth gap. Both wholesale and retail shifted from active to strengthening, though wholesale growth is nearly double retail's pace.
insights.implication: Trade finance is accelerating across both channels, but the wholesale advantage suggests businesses are building inventory and expanding operations faster than consumers are spending.

gaps.title: NBFCs command 34.1% of services credit and moved to strengthening status, while Trade…
gaps.body: NBFCs command 34.1% of services credit and moved to strengthening status, while Trade holds 22.7% and Other Services 21.0%. NBFCs advanced from active to strengthening status, widening their lead over other services subsectors.
gaps.implication: The NBFC boom is driving services credit expansion, as banks fund the non-bank lenders who reach segments traditional banks cannot serve efficiently.

insights.title: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% for 3 consecutive periods — the longest such…
insights.body: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% for 3 consecutive periods — the longest such streak on record. Extended from 2 periods to 3 periods, reaching the maximum streak length in this dataset.
insights.implication: Credit card demand has entered a sustained weak phase, suggesting consumer spending restraint is deepening rather than recovering.

insights.title: Credit cards now represent 4.24% of personal loans, down from 4.30% in the prior period
insights.body: Credit cards now represent 4.24% of personal loans, down from 4.30% in the prior period. Share declined 5.0% → 4.2% over the past year, hitting the lowest level in 6 periods.
insights.implication: Credit cards are losing ground within personal loans as weak growth causes market share erosion to faster-growing segments.

insights.title: Credit card outstanding grew 3.55% year-on-year in April 2026, up from 1.75% in March
insights.body: Credit card outstanding grew 3.55% year-on-year in April 2026, up from 1.75% in March. Growth collapsed 13.0% → 1.5% from February 2025 to February 2026, then recovered slightly to 3.5%.
insights.implication: The credit card sector shows early signs of bottoming out but remains in single-digit growth territory — a dramatic slowdown from double-digit norms.

insights.title: Consumer durables represent just 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period
insights.body: Consumer durables represent just 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period. Share dropped 0.4% → 0.3% over the past year, reaching the lowest level in this dataset.
insights.implication: Consumer durables have become a marginal segment within personal lending as the category remains in persistent decline.

insights.title: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, down slightly from 2.29% in the…
insights.body: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, down slightly from 2.29% in the prior period. Share has edged down 2.3% → 2.2% over the past year, reaching the minimum in this window.
insights.implication: Education loans maintain a stable but small share of personal lending, with slight erosion as other segments recover.

insights.title: Education loans grew 13.38% year-on-year, down from 13.72% in the prior period
insights.body: Education loans grew 13.38% year-on-year, down from 13.72% in the prior period. Growth has gradually decelerated 15.9% → 13.4% over the past year but remains in double digits.
insights.implication: Education loans are the standout performer in personal lending, delivering consistent double-digit growth as families prioritize skill investment despite economic uncertainty.

insights.title: Advances against Fixed Deposits reached 2.44% of personal loans, the highest level in…
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits reached 2.44% of personal loans, the highest level in this 6-period window. Share climbed from 2.27% to 2.44%, adding 0.17 percentage points.
insights.implication: This niche secured lending product is gaining ground as banks seek lower-risk exposure in personal loan portfolios.

insights.title: FD-backed advances surged 19.22% year-on-year, accelerating sharply from 14.07% growth…
insights.body: FD-backed advances surged 19.22% year-on-year, accelerating sharply from 14.07% growth last period. Growth trajectory jumped 14.07% → 19.22%, the fastest pace in this dataset.
insights.implication: Banks are aggressively expanding collateralised personal lending as a safer alternative to unsecured products.

insights.title: Gold loans have grown every period for 6 consecutive months, the longest unbroken run on…
insights.body: Gold loans have grown every period for 6 consecutive months, the longest unbroken run on record. Positive streak extended from 5 to 6 periods, marking a new maximum.
insights.implication: Gold lending has become the most reliable growth driver in personal loans, with no signs of momentum breaking.

insights.title: Gold loans captured 6.63% of all personal lending, near the peak of this 6-period range
insights.body: Gold loans captured 6.63% of all personal lending, near the peak of this 6-period range. Share rose 6.31% → 6.63%, continuing a dramatic climb from 3.0% a year ago.
insights.implication: Gold loans have doubled their portfolio weight in 12 months, reshaping the personal lending mix toward secured products.

insights.title: Gold loan growth held at 123.15%, slightly cooling from the 127.90% peak but remaining…
insights.body: Gold loan growth held at 123.15%, slightly cooling from the 127.90% peak but remaining above 120%. Growth eased 127.90% → 123.15%, still more than doubling year-on-year volumes.
insights.implication: Even with marginal deceleration, gold lending continues expanding at unprecedented rates as borrowers pledge jewelry for credit access.

insights.title: Housing loans fell to 48.35% of personal lending, the smallest share in this 6-period…
insights.body: Housing loans fell to 48.35% of personal lending, the smallest share in this 6-period window. Portfolio weight dropped 48.64% → 48.35%, extending a slide from over 50% a year ago.
insights.implication: Housing's traditional dominance is eroding as faster-growing secured products like gold capture market share.

insights.title: Housing loan growth strengthened to 11.48%, the best reading in this dataset
insights.body: Housing loan growth strengthened to 11.48%, the best reading in this dataset. Growth accelerated 10.98% → 11.48%, reaching the upper end of its recent range.
insights.implication: Despite losing share, housing credit is expanding at a healthy pace, suggesting robust underlying mortgage demand.

insights.title: Other personal loans held 24.99% of the portfolio, near the bottom of this period's range
insights.body: Other personal loans held 24.99% of the portfolio, near the bottom of this period's range. Share edged down 25.05% → 24.99%, continuing a gradual decline from 25.8% levels.
insights.implication: Miscellaneous unsecured lending is ceding ground to specialized secured products as risk appetite tightens.

insights.title: Other Personal Loans grew 12.98% year-on-year, the strongest reading in this 6-period…
insights.body: Other Personal Loans grew 12.98% year-on-year, the strongest reading in this 6-period window. Growth accelerated from 12.09% → 12.98%, building on a steady climb from 8.2% in February 2025.
insights.implication: Non-housing, non-vehicle personal credit is gaining momentum as banks expand into general consumer financing beyond traditional secured categories.

insights.title: Advances against shares and bonds held steady at 0.15% of total personal loans
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds held steady at 0.15% of total personal loans. Share remained flat at 0.15% from prior period, fluctuating within a narrow 0.1-0.2% band.
insights.implication: Securities-backed lending remains a niche product with minimal market penetration despite recent growth pickup.

insights.title: Advances against shares and bonds grew 4.63% year-on-year, the fastest pace in three…
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds grew 4.63% year-on-year, the fastest pace in three periods. Growth picked up from 2.82% → 4.63%, recovering from the sharp deceleration off 33% highs in early 2025.
insights.implication: Securities-backed lending is stabilising after the volatile swing from boom to near-stagnation, suggesting a more sustainable growth trajectory.

insights.title: Housing dominates personal loans at 48.3% share, followed by Other Personal Loans at…
insights.body: Housing dominates personal loans at 48.3% share, followed by Other Personal Loans at 25.0% and Vehicle Loans at 10.6%. The top three categories control 84% of the market, with niche segments like advances against shares (0.2%) and consumer durables (0.3%) representing minimal share.
insights.implication: Personal credit remains concentrated in housing and general-purpose loans, with secured vehicle financing the only other material segment — suggesting limited product diversification across banks.

insights.title: Vehicle loans held 10.64% of personal loans, essentially unchanged from the prior period
insights.body: Vehicle loans held 10.64% of personal loans, essentially unchanged from the prior period. Share slipped marginally from 10.68% → 10.64%, staying within the tight 10.4-10.7% range of recent periods.
insights.implication: Vehicle lending share is stable despite strong growth, indicating the broader personal loans pool is expanding at a similar pace.

insights.title: Vehicle loans grew 18.61% year-on-year, the fastest pace in this 6-period window
insights.body: Vehicle loans grew 18.61% year-on-year, the fastest pace in this 6-period window. Growth accelerated from 17.14% → 18.61%, more than doubling from the 8.6% low in April 2025.
insights.implication: Auto financing is in a pronounced upturn as consumer demand for vehicles rebounds and banks compete aggressively in this secured lending segment.

gaps.title: Consumer durables lending has contracted for 4 consecutive periods, the longest negative…
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending has contracted for 4 consecutive periods, the longest negative streak in this window. Negative streak extended from 3 periods to 4 periods, approaching the historical maximum of 5 periods.
gaps.implication: Consumer durables financing is in sustained contraction, reflecting weak demand for cars, appliances, and discretionary purchases.

gaps.title: Consumer durables lending contracted 5.34% year-on-year, an improvement from the 9.78%…
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending contracted 5.34% year-on-year, an improvement from the 9.78% decline in March. Contraction eased from -9.8% to -5.3%, but remains negative for the fourth straight period.
gaps.implication: While the pace of decline is moderating, consumer durables financing continues to shrink as discretionary spending remains weak.

gaps.title: Gold loans exploded 123.1% year-on-year, dwarfing all other personal loan categories,…
gaps.body: Gold loans exploded 123.1% year-on-year, dwarfing all other personal loan categories, while Consumer Durables contracted 5.3%. The growth spread widened to 128.5 percentage points between gold loans and consumer durables, with Vehicle Loans (18.6%) and Fixed Deposit advances (19.2%) also showing strong momentum.
gaps.implication: Personal credit is polarising sharply — secured lending against gold and vehicles is booming while unsecured consumer financing stagnates, reflecting banks' preference for collateral-backed exposure amid economic uncertainty.

insights.title: PSL agriculture credit grew 16.03% year-on-year, reaching its strongest pace in this…
insights.body: PSL agriculture credit grew 16.03% year-on-year, reaching its strongest pace in this 6-period window. Growth accelerated from 15.20% → 16.03%, building on a steady climb from 11.0% in April 2025.
insights.implication: Rural credit demand is gaining momentum as agricultural income improves, supporting both seasonal working capital and farm mechanisation lending.

insights.title: PSL educational loans expanded 23.02% year-on-year, maintaining near-peak growth despite…
insights.body: PSL educational loans expanded 23.02% year-on-year, maintaining near-peak growth despite a slight cooling. Growth eased from 23.96% → 23.02% after surging from near-zero levels a year ago.
insights.implication: Student loan demand remains robust as higher education costs rise, but banks may be tightening underwriting after the rapid expansion.

insights.title: PSL housing credit surged 39.80% year-on-year, hitting the highest growth rate in this…
insights.body: PSL housing credit surged 39.80% year-on-year, hitting the highest growth rate in this window. Growth strengthened from 38.25% → 39.80%, a dramatic turnaround from negative territory a year ago.
insights.implication: Affordable housing demand is booming as interest rates stabilise and government incentives drive first-time buyer activity.

insights.title: PSL medium enterprise credit grew 17.85% year-on-year, marking the slowest pace in this…
insights.body: PSL medium enterprise credit grew 17.85% year-on-year, marking the slowest pace in this 6-period window. Growth decelerated from 18.39% → 17.85%, down from peaks above 22% in early 2025.
insights.implication: Mid-market businesses are facing tighter credit conditions as banks shift focus to retail and micro-enterprise lending with better risk profiles.

insights.title: PSL MSME credit acceleration held at 16.1 percentage points on a full-year basis,…
insights.body: PSL MSME credit acceleration held at 16.1 percentage points on a full-year basis, maintaining maximum acceleration. The acceleration rate remained unchanged at 16.1 points across all periods, showing sustained momentum.
insights.implication: MSME lending has structurally shifted to a higher growth trajectory, driven by government guarantee schemes and digital lending platforms reducing friction.

insights.title: PSL micro and small enterprise credit expanded 29.53% year-on-year, reaching the…
insights.body: PSL micro and small enterprise credit expanded 29.53% year-on-year, reaching the strongest growth in this window. Growth accelerated from 26.49% → 29.53%, more than doubling from 13.0% levels a year ago.
insights.implication: Small business credit is experiencing a lending boom as digital platforms scale and government guarantee coverage reduces bank risk aversion.

insights.title: Renewable energy lending has grown for 6 consecutive periods, the longest positive streak…
insights.body: Renewable energy lending has grown for 6 consecutive periods, the longest positive streak on record. Extended from 5 to 6 periods, reaching the historical maximum.
insights.implication: Banks are sustaining their renewable energy push despite recent growth moderation, signaling structural commitment to green financing mandates.

insights.title: Renewable energy lending grew 34.13%, down from 64.36% in the prior period
insights.body: Renewable energy lending grew 34.13%, down from 64.36% in the prior period. Growth decelerated by 30.24 points, falling to the weakest reading in 6 periods.
insights.implication: The green lending boom is cooling after explosive growth phases, but double-digit expansion suggests the sector remains structurally attractive.

insights.title: Weaker sections lending grew 11.07%, down from 13.55% in the prior period
insights.body: Weaker sections lending grew 11.07%, down from 13.55% in the prior period. Growth slowed by 2.47 points, reaching the weakest level in 6 periods.
insights.implication: Financial inclusion momentum is softening as banks prioritize higher-yield PSL categories over mandated weaker section targets.

insights.title: Housing leads PSL growth at 39.8%, while export credit contracts 8.4% and others decline…
insights.body: Housing leads PSL growth at 39.8%, while export credit contracts 8.4% and others decline 9.6%. Spread of 49.4% between top and bottom performers, with 4 categories strengthening status.
insights.implication: PSL deployment is bifurcating sharply — housing and micro enterprises drive growth while trade-linked sectors struggle. Renewable energy and social infrastructure show renewed banker confidence.

gaps.title: PSL export credit contracted 8.36% year-on-year, though the decline is moderating from…
gaps.body: PSL export credit contracted 8.36% year-on-year, though the decline is moderating from deeper losses. Contraction narrowed from -14.09% → -8.36%, recovering from a low of -17.2% in February 2026.
gaps.implication: Export financing remains under pressure from global trade headwinds, but the improving trend suggests banks expect export recovery in coming quarters.

gaps.title: PSL other categories contracted 9.55% year-on-year, though the decline is the smallest in…
gaps.body: PSL other categories contracted 9.55% year-on-year, though the decline is the smallest in this window. Contraction narrowed sharply from -19.41% → -9.55%, recovering from a trough of -23.9% in February 2026.
gaps.implication: Specialty PSL categories are stabilising as banks complete portfolio rebalancing and focus on core agriculture, MSME, and housing segments.

gaps.title: Social infrastructure lending grew 12.88%, reversing from -1.02% contraction in the prior…
gaps.body: Social infrastructure lending grew 12.88%, reversing from -1.02% contraction in the prior period. Swung positive by 13.90 points, moving from decline to expansion.
gaps.implication: Banks are re-engaging with social infrastructure after a brief pullback, likely driven by government spending on healthcare and education facilities.

insights.title: Iron & Steel commands 68.8% of basic metal credit while Other Metal Products holds 31.2%
insights.body: Iron & Steel commands 68.8% of basic metal credit while Other Metal Products holds 31.2%. Iron & Steel strengthened its position while Other Metal Products weakened, creating a 37.7 percentage point spread between them.
insights.implication: Traditional steel is consolidating its credit dominance within metals, likely reflecting infrastructure and construction demand over specialty metal applications.

insights.title: Other Metal Products grew 21.0% year-on-year versus Iron & Steel at 18.7%
insights.body: Other Metal Products grew 21.0% year-on-year versus Iron & Steel at 18.7%. Growth rates are tightly clustered with just a 2.2 percentage point spread between the segments.
insights.implication: Despite Iron & Steel's share dominance, specialty metal products are expanding slightly faster, suggesting emerging demand beyond core infrastructure steel.

insights.title: Others leads chemical credit at 44.2%, followed by Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 33.9%, with…
insights.body: Others leads chemical credit at 44.2%, followed by Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 33.9%, with Petro Chemicals at 11.9% and Fertiliser at 9.9%. Drugs & Pharmaceuticals strengthened while Fertiliser weakened, with a 34.3 percentage point spread across the four segments.
insights.implication: Pharma is gaining credit allocation within chemicals while fertiliser loses ground, reflecting the shift toward higher-value chemical manufacturing over agricultural inputs.

insights.title: Petro Chemicals leads chemical growth at 27.7%, Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 17.9%, while…
insights.body: Petro Chemicals leads chemical growth at 27.7%, Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 17.9%, while Fertiliser contracts -4.5%. A 32.2 percentage point growth spread separates the fastest and slowest segments, with Fertiliser in outright decline.
insights.implication: Credit is flowing toward downstream petrochemicals and pharma manufacturing while agricultural fertiliser faces headwinds, marking a clear value-chain rotation within chemicals.

highlight: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels

insights.title: Other Engineering dominates at 81.1% of engineering credit while Electronics holds just…
insights.body: Other Engineering dominates at 81.1% of engineering credit while Electronics holds just 18.9%. Other Engineering strengthened its position while Electronics weakened, creating a 62.2 percentage point gap.
insights.implication: Traditional engineering sectors are capturing credit over electronics manufacturing, suggesting infrastructure and machinery demand outweighs tech hardware investment.

insights.title: Other Engineering grows 37.5% year-on-year versus Electronics at 13.3%
insights.body: Other Engineering grows 37.5% year-on-year versus Electronics at 13.3%. A 24.2 percentage point growth gap with Other Engineering significantly outpacing Electronics expansion.
insights.implication: Credit allocation favors heavy engineering and industrial machinery over electronics manufacturing, reflecting capital-intensive infrastructure buildout over consumer electronics.

insights.title: Others commands 75.7% of food processing credit, followed by Sugar at 11.9%, Edible Oils…
insights.body: Others commands 75.7% of food processing credit, followed by Sugar at 11.9%, Edible Oils at 10.4%, and Tea at 2.0%. Edible Oils strengthened while Others weakened, though Others maintains dominance with a 73.7 percentage point spread.
insights.implication: Specialty food processing segments like edible oils are gaining credit allocation within the broader food sector, suggesting value-addition trends in agricultural processing.

insights.title: Edible Oils & Vanaspati leads food processing growth at 23.9%, followed by Others at…
insights.body: Edible Oils & Vanaspati leads food processing growth at 23.9%, followed by Others at 14.8%, Sugar at 4.3%, and Tea at 0.8%. A 23.1 percentage point growth spread with Edible Oils significantly outperforming traditional segments like Sugar and Tea.
insights.implication: Credit is shifting toward processed food manufacturing and oil refining over traditional commodities like sugar and tea, reflecting consumer demand for packaged food products.

insights.title: Infrastructure dominates industry credit with a 32.6% share, followed by Basic Metal and…
insights.body: Infrastructure dominates industry credit with a 32.6% share, followed by Basic Metal and Metal Products at 11.3% and Other Industries at 7.6%. The top 3 segments control over half of industry lending, while bottom segments like Leather (0.3%) and Glass (0.4%) remain minimal players.
insights.implication: Industry credit is heavily concentrated in infrastructure build-out and core manufacturing. Banks are backing India's capital formation story through power, transport, and metals financing.

insights.title: Gems and Jewellery leads industry growth at 41.4%, followed by Petroleum/Coal at 32.5%…
insights.body: Gems and Jewellery leads industry growth at 41.4%, followed by Petroleum/Coal at 32.5% and All Engineering at 32.2%. Growth is broad-based with 7 active and 12 strengthening sectors, while laggards like Mining (7.2%) and Textiles (8.9%) still post positive growth.
insights.implication: The sector rotation is clear — banks are financing India's export push in gems, engineering goods, and energy transition. Even slow-growing traditional sectors remain in positive territory.

insights.title: Power commands 56.6% of infrastructure credit, with Roads at 22.6% and Other…
insights.body: Power commands 56.6% of infrastructure credit, with Roads at 22.6% and Other Infrastructure at 12.0%. The top 3 infrastructure categories control 91.2% of the segment, leaving transport modes like Airports (0.4%) and Railways (0.4%) as minimal shares.
insights.implication: Banks are heavily backing India's power sector expansion and road connectivity. The concentration suggests focused policy-driven lending rather than diversified infrastructure bets.

insights.title: Ports surge 48.7% while core infrastructure segments show mixed performance — Power up…
insights.body: Ports surge 48.7% while core infrastructure segments show mixed performance — Power up 22.1%, Roads barely positive at 1.1%. Transport infrastructure faces stress with Railways down 50.1%, Airports down 27.2%, and Telecoms down 12.2%.
insights.implication: Banks are financing India's export infrastructure (ports) aggressively while traditional transport sectors contract. This signals a shift toward trade-enabling infrastructure over domestic connectivity.

insights.title: Other Textiles dominates at 44.6% of textile credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5%
insights.body: Other Textiles dominates at 44.6% of textile credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5%. All four textile sub-segments remain active with a 43.0% spread between largest and smallest.
insights.implication: Textile lending is concentrated in non-cotton segments, likely reflecting synthetic fiber and technical textile expansion over traditional cotton manufacturing.

insights.title: All four textile segments posted positive growth, led by Jute Textiles at 16.5% and Other…
insights.body: All four textile segments posted positive growth, led by Jute Textiles at 16.5% and Other Textiles at 15.9%. Broad-based acceleration with Other Textiles and Cotton Textiles both upgrading to strengthening status.
insights.implication: Even traditional manufacturing sectors like textiles are benefiting from the credit expansion. The universal positive growth signals broad industrial optimism, not just tech or services.
